channel.title: Write that blog!
channel.description: Monthly dissection of engineering blog posts. Take a hint, get inspired, and write your own already.

item.title: Laguna XS.2 and M.1: A Deeper Dive
item.description: An accompanying set of benchmark results for Laguna large language models release. Target audience is mostly people who actually understand what "Nesterov momentum" means (not me), but I enjoyed this one despite my ignorance in the matter. Posting the exact environmental conditions for reproducing the benchmark results is an especially nice touch. Go Laguna XS!

item.title: Understanding the Go Runtime: The Network Poller
item.description: Last part of an educational series on Go language internals. Interesting especially for people who wondered how Go pulls off highly concurrent workloads if it clearly relies on a blocking I/O interface...

item.title: What are skiplists good for?
item.description: Introduction to a data structure that's often discussed, but seldom actually picked: skiplists. Perhaps expectedly, someone also quickly figured out that the "skip" prefix also works on trees.

item.title: Do You Even Need a Database?
item.description: Honest dissection of approaches to storing data. tl;dr not using any database can get you surprisingly far, but then why not just slap an SQLite on it.

item.title: Open source security at Astral
item.description: Creators of uv share their take on open-source security in the age of AI. Lots of interesting rabbit-hole links, explaining security aspects like impostor commits, unpinned uses, immutable releases, dependency cooldowns, and more.

item.title: The Git Commands I Run Before Reading Any Code
item.description: Git magic at its finest. This article is a concise list of git commands useful for opinionated code inspection. Worth saving.

item.title: 10x faster tokenization
item.description: zml folks power-vibe-rewrote their tokenizer to Zig to drop the foreign language dependencies, and get a modest 10x speedup along the way. Going Rust-free must feel good indeed.

item.title: How Meta Used AI to Map Tribal Knowledge in Large-Scale Data Pipelines
item.description: Meta's recipe for helping AI agents navigate huge repositories. Useful philosophy, and quite easy to apply to your code, large or not.

item.title: VHDL's crown jewel;
item.description: Turns out the difference between VHDL and Verilog is not "just syntax!" VHDL makes sure that events that happen concurrently in a "zero time" window are still simulated in a deterministic way. And determinism is a thing to be vastly appreciated in a hardware debugging world.

item.title: When Agents Have Wallets
item.description: An interesting projection on what the world is going to look like when we start giving AI agents pocket money.

item.title: Hypothesis, Antithesis, synthesis
item.description: Introduction to property testing, along with an open-source multilingual tool to put it in practice.

item.title: What Happens with Open Source in the Age of AI?
item.description: Welcome to March 2026: the month of questioning Open Source paradigm in the age of AI. This one is quite an optimistic take on the topic, for a change!

item.title: How We Hacked McKinsey's AI Platform
item.description: Terminator-style white hat hacker story on how an autonomous agentic AI job penetrated McKinsley's platform and leaked all its data. The future is now.

item.title: AI And The Ship of Theseus
item.description: A philosophical take on GPL-ish licenses in the era of AI. Free Internet dramas inside!

item.title: Nobody ever got fired for using a struct
item.description: Valuable tips for everybody struggling to squeeze a bloated database row into a structure in memory, while not keeping 150 NULL values around at all times.

item.title: Never snooze a future
item.description: Words of warning against a novel problem that emerged from async Rust -- cancel safety. Rule of thumb: futures can be implicitly "cancelled" by just not ever polling their status anymore, so it's important to never leave important (or even worse, temporarily invalid) state hanging in them.

item.title: Apache Iggy's migration journey to thread-per-core architecture powered by io_uring
item.description: February longest title award granted! It is quite to the point though. A good set of conclusions and results on how best to apply io_uring and CPU sharding to a large Rust project.

item.title: Look ma, no FUSE!
item.description: (shameless self-promo, I enjoyed writing this one more than usual though)

item.title: Farewell, Rust
item.description: False alarm, the language is still alive, and so is Rust Foundation. What ended is the author's eagerness to use it for web development, with quite a few valid points.

item.title: Catching a caching bug at Readyset
item.description: Antithesis demoes a real scenario of debugging a bug in an SQL cache, through fuzzing, time travel, and exploring multiverses.

item.title: How to build a distributed queue in a single JSON file on object storage
item.description: Simplest idea wins. A fantastic crash course of how a single JSON file laying around somewhere in an S3 bucket can be a full-fledged distributed queue with optimistic locking on top.

item.title: six thoughts on generating c
item.description: A neat package of six patterns to follow when generating C language snippets. The code snippets and practical references help a lot.

item.title: Owning a $5M data center
item.description: comma.ai's recipe for running your own datacenter the oldschool way. Tips and tricks on energy and cooling included.

item.title: Learning from context is harder than we thought
item.description: An interesting approach to benchmarking large language models. Its main conclusion is that at the time of writing, best models still struggle with real-time learning from latest context -- something that humans tend to excel at.

item.title: Building a Movie Recommendation Agent
item.description: Actually practical walkthrough on building an actually helpful agent (specifically two of them, so an "agent orchestrator," but not of the unhinged gas town quality). Easy to follow and get inspired to build one for your own use case.

item.title: Why does SSH send 100 packets per keystroke?
item.description: A machine-enhanced investigation on throughput and latency issues in an SSH game. Quite educational on SSH protocol quirks.

item.title: Replacing Protobuf with Rust to go 5 times faster
item.description: A story on rewriting serialization routines to Rust, while keeping the protobuf interface intact. Bonus: interesting take on recursive algorithms and their interaction with CPU caches (the L1/L2/... kind).

item.title: ASCII characters are not pixels: a deep dive into ASCII rendering
item.description: A must-read. The author decided to spend a fair amount of time creating a comprehensive tutorial on rendering 3D graphics with ASCII characters. With visuals, code snippets, and all. Go read it and find out that ASCII rendering is a branch of science in its own right.

item.title: The Design & Implementation of Sprites
item.description: The article describes a quite novel approach to disposable computers, which is a term lately coined by fly.io. Like sandboxes, but with custom containers replaced with persistence and speed.

item.title: Database Transactions
item.description: Practical and interactive summary of what database transactions are. Comes with a complimentary rundown on isolation levels.

item.title: Don't fall into the anti-AI hype
item.description: A nice counterweight to all the, well, anti-AI hype.

item.title: See it with your lying ears
item.description: Extremely educational piece on audio editing, including a 20-line implementation of fast Fourier transform and lots of audio+video samples. Also includes neat tricks on how to make the output more feasible when naïve approaches subtly fail.

item.title: Finding and Fixing Ghostty's Largest Memory Leak
item.description: An investigation of memory leaks in the world's most hyped terminal emulator's scrolling implementation. Includes practical debugging techniques like ARM memory tags (TIL).

item.title: Databases in 2025: A Year in Review
item.description: Everything you need to know about the state of databases in 2025. Delievered in a uniquely entertaining way.

item.title: Swapping two blocks of memory that reside inside a larger block, in constant memory
item.description: The title kind of says it all.

item.title: Writing mutexes from scratch in Go
item.description: Another hands-on tutorial on implementing a mutex, through spinlocks, process schedulers, and finally the grand idea of futex. Very nice summary, and the part with using Go explicit coroutine scheduling is a nice touch.

item.title: Software engineers should be a little bit cynical
item.description: An ideal cynical essay on why cynical approach is an ideal counterweight to idealism.
Succinct title award for 2025 (in both senses). A blog post on writing blog posts!

item.title: Writing a blatant Telegram clone using Qt, QML and Rust. And C++.
item.description: A story of writing a Telegram clone, including tiniest details like properly sizing rows of emojis. Impressive meticulousness, as well as the end result!

item.title: A disappearing Service Processor
item.description: It's a bug hunt post with FPGA mentioned at least 7 times. No more recommendation needed.

item.title: How a Kernel Bug Froze My Machine: Debugging an Async-profiler Deadlock
item.description: December is rife with low-level bug hunts, what a perfect Christmas gift for everyone! This time, it's a Linux kernel issue plus a nice stuntman trick of reviving a deadlocked kernel with gdb.

item.title: Do Not Optimize Away
item.description: An interesting take on using the blackbox trick for microbenchmarks, or actually not using it.

item.title: Prediction: AI will make formal verification go mainstream
item.description: A prediction that hopefully comes true. Since almost everyone adores TLA+, and yet almost nobody is capable of writing a single correct line of it, perhaps we really need to offload formal verification to AI.

item.title: Your intellectual fly is open
item.description: A much needed call to stop posting LLM-generated content everywhere, because it shows. AI is great for many things, but not that. Have confidence in your own writing!

item.title: Why We Built Lightpanda in Zig
item.description: List of reasons for picking Zig over other system languages to build a browser for agents. Quite a standard collection of Zig's advantages, accompanied by nice short code snippets.

item.title: In defense of lock poisoning in Rust
item.description: An educational essay on the advantages of poisoning. In this particular case the poison is used in a homeopathy-style therapy, to prevent more serious issues from happening.

item.title: Imgur Geo-Blocked the UK, So I Geo-Unblocked My Entire Network
item.description: Beautiful tutorial on how to selectively route only specific websites through VPN, while keeping the rest of your network fast. Especially useful in the age of geo-blocking everything.

item.title: Assert in production
item.description: Good conclusions from the Cloudflare unwrap fiasco. tl;dr your production code should crash more, not less, especially if the alternative is running in a defective state. Much better to suffer from a few hours of downtime than chasing your leaked personal data in the darkest corners of the Internet.

item.title: Intel is listening, don't waste your shot
item.description: This article dares you to share brutally honest feedback with hardware vendors, for everyone's benefit. (And admits it is easier said that done.) Interesting perspective from an author who has been on both sides of those meetings with vendors.
